Sorry -- would you, or will you? I lost that one.
Hudson Travel, this is Nathan. So -- September. Tell me the trip you actually want, not the one you think you should book.
Five days, and they are mine. If I had one perfect week in New York, I would spend it walking. Not one museum a day -- one neighborhood a day.
Good. Then I'd put you in Brooklyn for two nights. It's off the beaten path for most of my clients, and you'd still be twenty minutes from Manhattan.
The line is bad -- let me read that back to you: two nights in Brooklyn, twenty minutes from Manhattan. Is that right?
That's right. Now, the flight. If you took the red-eye on Saturday, you'd land at seven in the morning and you'd gain a whole day.
I would rather arrive early and be tired than lose a Sunday. Let's take the red-eye. What would that do to the budget?
It saves you about two hundred dollars. If I were you, I'd put that money into the food, not into the room. Most people splurge on the hotel, and then they eat badly for five days.
I like that. If we cut back on the hotel, we'd stretch the budget into two great dinners -- and I'd keep one evening completely open.
Open for what? Most of my clients want every hour on the calendar.
Open for the thing I haven't planned. The best night of my last trip was completely spontaneous -- a detour, really. I wish I planned less and looked more. So this time, one evening stays empty.
If I were you, I'd take the ferry instead of the train.
I would rather arrive early and be tired than lose a whole day.
If we cut back on the hotel, we could stretch the budget into two great dinners.

Hi Lucimara, this is Nathan from Hudson Travel, returning your call about September. I have put together two versions of that week, and honestly, they are very different. Version one is the safe one. You land at Kennedy on Sunday, you stay in Midtown, and everything is ten minutes from the hotel. If I were you, I would not take that one. Version two costs almost the same, but it puts you in Brooklyn for two nights. If you stayed in Brooklyn, you would see the part of the city that most of my clients never see, and you would still be twenty minutes from your meeting. Now, about the flight. If you took the red-eye on Saturday, you would land at seven in the morning and you would gain a whole day. If you would rather sleep in a real bed, take the Sunday flight, and we lose Sunday. One last thing. If I had your budget, I would not splurge on the room. I would cut back on the hotel and put the money into the food. Call me back and tell me which version sounds like you.

Welcome back to The Slow Traveler. Today I want to ask you the question I ask every guest, and this week I am going to answer it myself. If you had seven free days, and money was not the problem, where would you go, and what would you actually do? Here is my honest answer. I would not go anywhere new. I would go back to New York, and I would do it completely differently. I wish I were braver when I travel. I wish I had more days and fewer plans. So this time, if I had that week, I would give myself one rule and only one: one neighborhood a day, on foot, off the beaten path. I would take the ferry instead of the train, because the ferry is a detour, and detours are where the trip actually happens. I would eat where the line is longest. And if a stranger invited me somewhere, I would say yes. My producer says that is not a plan, that is a mood. She would rather have a spreadsheet. But I think the best week of your life is never the one you organized. It is the one you were brave enough to leave open.
